_meta.purpose: Sub-state income taxes: city, county, school district and special-district levies that sit on top of state income tax. Researched July 2026 against jurisdiction and state revenue sources.
_meta.whyItMatters: Our audience is mostly self-employed, and the local rule for SELF-EMPLOYMENT NET PROFIT is frequently different from the wage rule. Every entry records appliesToSelfEmploymentNetProfit separately for that reason, and null there means 'not confirmed' — never 'no'.
shapes: percentage of state taxable income (Maryland counties, Indiana counties)
percentage of gross wages or net profit (Kentucky, Michigan, Missouri, Delaware, Alabama)
percentage OF THE STATE TAX rather than of income (Yonkers resident surcharge)
flat dollars per week worked, not a rate at all (West Virginia city service fees)
flat dollars per year (Portland Arts Tax)
threshold levies on income above a floor (Oregon Metro SHS, Multnomah PFA)
_meta.status: All 12 local-tax states captured. Ohio and Pennsylvania completed last, and are the most complex by a distance.
_meta.crossChecked: Indiana, Kentucky and Alabama were researched twice independently. The second pass RESOLVED Alabama's open question — the occupational tax does not reach self-employment profit — and resolved Kentucky's contested school levy. It also surfaced that Indiana's 2026 STATE rate is 2.95%, not the 3.00% in the legacy dataset, which is now corrected in state-brackets-2026.json. Michigan, Missouri, Delaware, West Virginia and Oregon were also researched twice. The second pass confirmed Saginaw's rate against the city's own form and identified the widely-used Grand Rapids aggregate table as stale; corrected Delaware's head tax from the commonly quoted $10 to $15; found Weirton's 2026 increase from $2 to $5 and Fairmont's inverted resident rule; and established that Oregon's statewide transit tax stayed at 0.1% because Measure 120 failed in May 2026, contradicting many payroll guides. Michigan was then researched a THIRD time to verify the 20 cities that had rested on the statutory default: all 20 confirmed 1%/0.5% from their own pages or forms, so the whole state is now high confidence. That pass also found two more bad sources — Portland's and Muskegon Heights' instruction PDFs both carry copy-paste boilerplate quoting Grand Rapids' 1.5/0.75 alongside their own correct 1%/0.5%. Scraping either would have produced wrong rates.
_meta.fetchBlocked: michigan.gov, kcmo.gov and wilmingtonde.gov all refuse automated fetching. Figures for those came from alternative official sources — Michigan Treasury form PDFs, the St. Louis Comptroller's comparison page, and Wilmington's own budget portal.

nycResidentTax.appliesTo: NYC residents and part-year residents only
nycResidentTax.source: tax.ny.gov IT-201-I NYC rate schedule; NYSDTF confirmed no NYC change for 2026

nycUnincorporatedBusinessTax.note: A separate 4% tax on unincorporated businesses with NYC-source income — sole proprietors and partnerships. Unlike the personal income tax it DOES reach nonresidents. Exemptions and the resident credit were not researched; do not model without further work.
nycUnincorporatedBusinessTax.source: nyc.gov Department of Finance UBT page

yonkersResidentSurcharge.note: 16.75% OF your New York STATE income tax, not of income. A rate on a tax, which no other jurisdiction in this file does.

NY._notes: NYC has no nonresident personal income tax — the commuter tax was repealed in 1999. But the 4% UBT does hit nonresident sole proprietors with NYC-source income, which is the trap for our audience.

MD.shape: percentage of Maryland taxable income, set by COUNTY OF RESIDENCE not county of work
MD.source: marylandcomptroller.gov 2026 state and local withholding memo, Attachment 1
MD._notes: Mandatory for every Maryland resident, levied on the same base as the state tax so it automatically reaches self-employment profit, capital gains and interest. Allegany rose 3.03->3.20 and Kent 3.20->3.30 for 2026.

IN.shape: percentage of Indiana adjusted gross income — same base as the state tax
IN.nonresidentRule: County is fixed as of January 1. An out-of-state resident on January 1 whose principal place of work or business is in an Indiana county pays that county's FULL rate — there is no longer a reduced nonresident rate.
IN._notes: All 92 counties levy it, on the same base as the state tax, so it reaches self-employment profit in full. Porter is the cheapest at 0.5% and Randolph the dearest at 3.0% — a six-fold spread between neighbouring counties. Six counties changed rates effective January 1, 2026: Carroll, Grant, Greene, Howard, Shelby, Union. The odd non-round values (Brown 0.025234, Carroll 0.024733, Jasper 0.02864, Whitley 0.016829) are exact as published and must not be rounded. Re-pull dn01.pdf each January. Confirmed twice, independently, from the same DOR notice.

KY.shape: occupational license fee on gross wages AND net profits, levied by cities, counties and some school districts

Louisville / Jefferson County.note: The 0.75% school-board component does not apply to nonresident individuals, hence the lower nonresident rate. Confirmed from TY2024/25 official form instructions; louisvilleky.gov blocks automated fetch so no 2026-specific confirmation.

Fayette County Public Schools.note: RESOLVED by a second independent pass: the increase to 0.75% was declared unlawful and VOID by Kentucky Attorney General opinion OAG 25-07, for failure to give the required week of public notice. The rate in force remains 0.5%, stacking on LFUCG's 2.25% for a combined 2.75%. Confidence medium — FCPS's own page carries no year stamp, and SB 76 was filed in January 2026 to restrict such levies further.

KY._notes: These reach net profits of sole proprietors and contractors explicitly, with no personal exemptions or deductions. Both a city and a county may tax the same income, though counties over 30,000 population must credit city tax paid.
KY.stackingRule: KRS 68.197 requires a credit against the COUNTY tax for amounts paid to a city within that county, where the county has 30,000+ population. Stacking a city rate and a county rate without applying the credit overstates liability — this is the single easiest way to get Kentucky wrong.

AL.shape: municipal occupational tax on gross wages for work performed in the city
AL._notes: RESOLVED by a second independent pass, and the answer is no. Alabama's occupational tax is a WAGE tax: Birmingham's ordinance keys it to compensation where 'the relationship between the individual performing the services and the person for whom such services are rendered is the legal relationship of employer and employee'. Self-employed people are instead reached by the separate municipal BUSINESS LICENSE tax on gross receipts, which is a different levy with different rates by classification. Do NOT apply Alabama occupational tax to Schedule C profit. Auburn and Opelika confirmed from their own city sites; the remaining ~22 cities come from an undated Alabama League of Municipalities survey and are low confidence. Act 2020-14 freezes NEW occupational taxes without legislative approval, so the roster is stable, but existing cities can still change their rate.

MI.shape: city income tax under the Uniform City Income Tax Ordinance
MI.generalRule: Nonresident rate is one-half the resident rate by statute, and nonresidents are taxed only on income earned inside the city. Residents pay on all income wherever earned.
MI._notes: 24 cities levy a city income tax, ALL NOW VERIFIED from the city's own page or form. Statutory default is 1% resident / 0.5% nonresident (MCL 141.611), with the nonresident rate never exceeding half the resident rate. Four exceptions, each with its own population-gated statutory hook: Detroit 2.4/1.2, Highland Park 2.0/1.0, Grand Rapids 1.5/0.75, Saginaw 1.5/0.75. None of the other 20 qualifies for a higher-rate provision, so 1%/0.5% is their ceiling as well as their rate. SELF-EMPLOYMENT: taxed both ways. MCL 141.612(b) taxes a resident owner's distributive share of unincorporated business profits WHEREVER CONDUCTED; MCL 141.613(b) taxes a nonresident's only to the extent CONDUCTED IN THE CITY, apportioned by the city's business allocation formula. Pensions, annuities and Social Security are not subject. EAST LANSING SUNSETS after tax year 2030 — its tax was authorized for 12 years from January 2019 and is the only one here with an expiry, so it cannot be treated as permanent.

statutoryDefault.authority: MCL 141.611; the nonresident rate may never exceed half the resident rate

statutoryAuthority.default: MCL 141.611 — 1% resident / 0.5% nonresident, and the nonresident rate may never exceed half the resident rate
statutoryAuthority.Detroit: MCL 141.503(2) — population over 600,000; max 2.40/1.20, dropping to 2.20/1.10 once lighting-authority debt is retired

sourceTraps: Portland's 'General Instructions' PDF contains a copy-paste error reading .015/.0075 — boilerplate inherited from the CF common-form template, which carries Grand Rapids' rates. Portland's own P-1040 and rate table both say 1.00/0.50.
Muskegon Heights' booklet has the same inherited boilerplate line alongside its own correct rate. The operative rate is 1%/0.5%.
The all-cities table on grandrapidsmi.gov is stale on Saginaw. Do not use any of these three as a primary source.

MO.shape: 1% earnings tax on wages and on net profits of businesses and self-employed persons
MO._notes: Both were renewed by voters on April 7, 2026 for a further five years, so both are confirmed in force. Residents pay on all earnings wherever performed; nonresidents pay on work actually performed inside the city and can claim a refund for out-of-city days. Both explicitly reach net profits, so a sole proprietor pays 1% on city-attributable profit. St. Louis's 0.5% payroll expense tax is employer-side and is not part of an individual's calculation.

DE.shape: flat percentage on gross wages, plus a parallel net profits tax at the same rate

Wilmington.headTaxNote: $15 per employee per month on businesses with 6+ employees, assessed on headcount minus 5. The widely quoted $10 is STALE. It is an EMPLOYER tax and must never be deducted from a paycheck calculation.

DE._notes: Wilmington is Delaware's only municipal wage tax, confirmed from the City's own FY2027 budget tax-rate sheet and unchanged across FY2025-FY2027. 1.25% on gross earned income, paid at the SAME rate by residents and by nonresidents working in the city — unlike Michigan there is no half-rate for nonresidents. A separate Net Profits Tax, also 1.25%, covers sole proprietors and partnerships. The rate is set by the Delaware General Assembly, not the city.

WV.shape: flat dollars per week worked — NOT a percentage, and must never be modeled as one
WV.appliesToSelfEmploymentNetProfit: n/a as a rate; self-employed people working in the city are liable for the flat weekly fee, confirmed for Huntington

Weirton.note: RAISED FROM $2.00 IN 2026 — Ordinance 2272 passed second reading April 14, 2026, effective about mid-May. Confirmed by news reporting only; the official ordinance PDF is an unreadable scan. Most aggregators still say $2.00.

Charleston.note: $3.00 since January 2018. Some sources quote '$6 per pay period' — that is the biweekly equivalent, not a different rate.

Morgantown.note: An increase was under discussion through May 2026 but had not been adopted. Recheck before shipping.

Wheeling.note: Source document dated 2019, effective January 2020. No later change found, but no 2026-dated confirmation either.

Fairmont.note: INVERTS THE USUAL PATTERN. Only NONRESIDENT employees pay the weekly payroll charge; Fairmont residents are exempt from it and instead pay $8.67 a month on their city services bill.

WV._notes: A different shape from every other entry: a fixed dollar amount per week worked, regardless of hours or earnings. Anyone working at a physical location in the city pays, residents and commuters alike. Annual cost is roughly the amount times weeks worked. Not an exhaustive list of WV cities.
WV.namingTrap: Match on the fee's BASE, not its name. Huntington has two: the CITY Service Fee is the $5/week payroll fee, while its MUNICIPAL Service Fee is a property-owner fire fee and is not a wage item at all. Other cities use 'Municipal Service Fee' to mean the payroll fee.
WV.authority: W. Va. Code 8-13-13, special charges for municipal services

OR.shape: threshold levies on Oregon taxable income above a floor, plus one flat annual charge

Metro Supportive Housing Services.note: Thresholds rose from $125,000/$200,000 because Metro Council adopted CPI indexing effective January 1, 2026; the rate did not change. Metro publishes updated thresholds by November 15 each year.

Multnomah County Preschool for All.note: The scheduled +0.8 percentage-point increase DOES NOT APPLY IN 2026 — it was delayed by Board ordinance to January 1, 2027. For 2026 the rates remain 1.5% and 3.0%. Thresholds are NOT indexed, unlike SHS.

Portland Arts Tax.note: A flat annual charge, not a rate, and not prorated for part-year residents. Payable by Portland residents 18 or over with $1,000 or more of income, above the federal poverty level.

OR._notes: Both Metro SHS and Multnomah PFA use Oregon taxable income, so a sole proprietor's Schedule C profit counts toward the thresholds and is taxed. Both reach NONRESIDENTS on district-sourced income — the well-known trap for Washington-state commuters who owe Metro SHS on Portland-source income despite living in a no-income-tax state. The statewide transit tax and TriMet/Lane payroll taxes are employer-side or statewide and are excluded.
OR.remoteWorkRule: A nonresident who physically travels into the district to work IS taxable on that work. A nonresident employed by a district employer who works ENTIRELY REMOTELY from outside it — the Vancouver, Washington home office — is NOT. This is the distinction that decides the answer for most cross-river commuters and should be modeled explicitly rather than assumed.

statewideTransitTax.note: STAYED AT 0.1% FOR 2026. The 2025 transportation package would have doubled it to 0.2% from January 1, 2026, but Measure 120 was DEFEATED at the May 19, 2026 primary, so DOR instructs employers to continue withholding 0.1%. Many payroll-vendor 2026 guides still say 0.2% and are wrong.

employerTaxesNotPersonal.note: Employer-side, not part of an individual's calculation — but both have parallel SELF-EMPLOYMENT versions charged on net earnings over $400 from services in the district, which are.

OH.shape: municipal income tax on wages and net profit, PLUS a separate additive school district income tax
OH.statutoryNote: ORC 718.04(G): no municipality may exceed 1% without majority voter approval. Once approved there is NO ceiling — the highest rates in the state exceed 3%.

baseIsNotStateTaxableIncome.note: Municipalities are statutorily barred from taxing retirement income. A retiree in Columbus with $80,000 of pension income owes $0 municipal tax. This base is unlike any other state's in the file.

OH.businessIncomeDeductionNote: THE BIGGEST SURPRISE FOR OWNER-OPERATORS: Ohio's state Business Income Deduction — the $250,000 / 3% break — does NOT apply at the municipal level. Schedule C profit is fully municipally taxable, both where the work was performed and where the owner lives.
OH.creditRule: The residence municipality taxes all a resident's income wherever earned; the workplace municipality taxes it at source; the residence city then grants a credit. STATE LAW MANDATES NO CREDIT — each city sets its own credit percentage AND its own credit rate limit by ordinance.
OH.creditCapsAreNotUniform: A calculator that assumes a full credit UNDERSTATES liability for six of the twenty largest cities.

Springfield.creditNote: 50% credit, additionally capped at 50% of Springfield's own tax. A resident working elsewhere ALWAYS owes Springfield at least 1.2%.

Lakewood.creditNote: Credit capped at a 0.5% rate against a 1.5% tax. A Lakewood resident working elsewhere still owes Lakewood 1.0%.

Middletown.creditNote: UNRESOLVED — the ordinance implies a 100% credit up to 2.0% but the current return form still prints 'not to exceed 1.75%'. Left null; needs a phone call to the city.

Newark.creditNote: Credit capped at 1.0% against a 1.75% tax; a resident working elsewhere still owes 0.75%.

Mansfield.creditNote: Credit capped at 1.0% of gross wage per W-2, residents only; a resident working elsewhere still owes 1.25%.

schoolDistrictIncomeTax.administeredBy: Ohio Department of Taxation, filed on form SD 100 with the STATE return — not by RITA or CCA
schoolDistrictIncomeTax.trigger: residency in the district on the last day of the year. Workplace is irrelevant.
schoolDistrictIncomeTax.districtsNote: parsed from ODT's official 2026 withholding list; secondary sources say roughly 210. Approximate.

earnedIncomeBase.note: wages, salaries, tips and net self-employment earnings ONLY — no exemptions, no deductions

OH._notes: Ohio is the most complex local-tax state in the country and the traps are structural, not arithmetic. FOUR things a calculator must get right. (1) The base is NOT state taxable income — no pensions, no Social Security, no individual investment income. (2) Self-employment IS fully taxed and the state Business Income Deduction does NOT apply municipally, which is the single most common surprise for owner-operators. (3) Credit caps are not uniform: six of the twenty largest cities cap the credit BELOW their own rate, so assuming a full credit understates liability — a Lakewood resident working elsewhere still owes 1.0%, a Springfield resident at least 1.2%. (4) School district tax is a SEPARATE additive tax filed with the state, triggered by residency alone, and half the districts use a base that taxes retirement income while the other half do not.

PA.shape: Act 32 Earned Income Tax everywhere except Philadelphia, which runs its own wage and net profits taxes, plus a flat-dollar Local Services Tax
PA.confidence: high on mechanics, medium on rate ranges

eit.residentRule: home municipality rate + home school district rate, on all earned income and net profits wherever earned
eit.nonresidentRule: the worksite municipality's non-resident rate, which is the municipal share only — school districts cannot levy on non-residents
eit.withholdingRule: Act 32 makes the employer withhold the GREATER of the employee's resident rate or the worksite non-resident rate, and remit to the employee's HOME collection district. That is why most employees never see a bill.
eit.selfEmploymentNote: Sole proprietors, single-member LLCs and generally individual partners owe EIT on Schedule C and K-1 ordinary business income, and must file QUARTERLY ESTIMATES directly with their collector. No employer withholds it, so it is routinely missed.
eit.sCorpNote: S-CORP DISTRIBUTIVE SHARE IS NOT SUBJECT TO PA LOCAL EIT. This is the largest planning difference for Pennsylvania business owners and a common calculator error — it makes the S-corp election worth materially more in PA than the federal maths alone suggests.
eit.act511Cap: Combined municipal + school EIT is generally capped at 1% unless separately authorized by home rule charter, Act 1 referendum or distressed-city status. Where the cap binds and both levy, the split is typically 0.5/0.5.

philadelphia.rateChangeDate: July 1, NOT January 1 — a calendar-2026 calculation must blend two rates

netProfitsTax.flag: NOT CONFIRMED FOR 2026. The convention is that the NPT rate for a tax year equals the wage tax rate effective July 1 of that year, which gives these figures, but no official City page stating the 2026 NPT rate was found. Do not ship without confirming. NPT filers generally also owe BIRT, with a partial credit.

philadelphia.note: The Wage Tax does not cover the self-employed at all — they pay the separate Net Profits Tax, and usually BIRT on top.

pittsburgh.nonresidentRule: the 1% city non-resident rate only; the 2% school share does not reach non-residents
pittsburgh.flag: Confirmed from consistent institutional sources only — pittsburghpa.gov refuses automated fetching and the DCED register is an ASPX postback.

localServicesTax.shape: flat annual dollars, not a percentage
localServicesTax.aggregateCap: $52 total per taxpayer per year across all subdivisions worked in; the taxpayer claims a refund for any excess
localServicesTax.exemptionRule: Any subdivision levying more than $10 a year MUST exempt anyone with under $12,000 of earned income and net profits from sources within it.
localServicesTax.selfEmploymentNote: The self-employed owe it on the same earned-income-and-net-profits basis and, where the rate exceeds $10, must pay QUARTERLY. There is no employer to withhold it, so sole proprietors routinely miss it.

PA.officialRegister: munstats.pa.gov FindLocalTax and the DCED Official Tax Register
PA._notes: Two systems that do not talk to each other. Act 32 EIT covers the whole state except Philadelphia, which sits outside it entirely with its own wage tax, its own net profits tax for the self-employed, and rates that change on 1 JULY rather than January 1 — so any calendar-2026 figure blends two rates. The single most valuable fact here for our audience: S-corp distributive share is NOT subject to PA local EIT, while sole-proprietor net profit is. In a jurisdiction at 3% combined that is a real reason to elect, and it is invisible in the federal calculation. The LST is a flat dollar tax the self-employed owe quarterly with nobody withholding it.
